---
title: "Semantic Representation and Tree-Structured Models"
chapter_id: "semantic-representation-and-tree-structured-models"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "treellm"
  - "spinoza"
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "cgs"
  - "circle_ai_architectures"
  - "coilra_multicoil_rope"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact passage-reviewed mappings for all seven assigned semantic-representation sources; support remains at argument until grounding, hierarchy-revision, representation-utility, model-quality, or interoperability tests exist."
  - "The semantic_node_record fixture validates schema/example consistency only; no TreeLLM implementation, semantic-token benchmark, grounding evaluation, hierarchy-revision harness, cyclic-substrate experiment, or model-quality benchmark has been run."
  - "TreeLLM source-reported model-size, grounding, reasoning, benchmark, superiority, smartphone-scale, and final-architecture claims are not reproduced here and are treated only as source-reported representation-proposal context."
  - "Circle/Coil mappings are passage-reviewed against public source notes only because raw local project text is not copied into this repo; they support optional-substrate guardrails, not quality or performance claims."
  - "No support-state promotion, raw source publication, semantic graph adequacy result, or cyclic-model advantage is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `semantic-representation-and-tree-structured-models` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `treellm`, `spinoza`, `verification_bandwidth`, `cognitive_compilation`, `cgs`, `circle_ai_architectures`, `coilra_multicoil_rope` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `treellm`; supporting: `spinoza`, `verification_bandwidth`, `cognitive_compilation`, `cgs`, `circle_ai_architectures`, `coilra_multicoil_rope` |
| Source loading state | source notes and passage-reviewed local raw-cache mappings exist for `treellm`, `spinoza`, `verification_bandwidth`, `cognitive_compilation`, and `cgs`; `circle_ai_architectures` and `coilra_multicoil_rope` are passage-reviewed against public source notes only because raw local project text is not copied into this repo. |
| Test state | `semantic_node_record.valid.json` and repository-level semantic fixtures pass protocol validation; `AsiStackProofs.SemanticRepresentation` implements finite grounded-provenance and hierarchy-update predicates; grounding, hierarchy-revision, and representation-utility tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats TreeLLM as a representation proposal, not a proven model-size, reasoning, or benchmark improvement. The repository has no TreeLLM implementation, semantic-token benchmark, or graph-grounding evaluation.

Following artifact compression, semantic representation shifts the compression question from bytes to meaning. A tree or graph can make concepts easier to route and inspect, but only if provenance, permitted use, adequacy limits, hierarchy changes, and residual uncertainty remain attached.

The chapter's central discipline is semantic leasing. A semantic node is allowed to represent a concept for a bounded purpose, under a declared grounding state, with reviewable provenance and a revocation or supersession path. It is not a permanent claim that the system has captured the concept itself.

## Problem

The ASI Stack needs representations that are easier to inspect, update, route, and verify than opaque token continuations alone. A model can generate fluent claims while hiding which concepts, sources, relations, and assumptions it used. Tree-structured or graph-structured representations are attractive because they can expose paths, provenance, hierarchy, and residual attributes.

But explicit structure can also become false confidence. A semantic graph can be incomplete, biased, brittle, stale, or treated as an unreviewed authority source. The problem is to use semantic structure as an optional substrate with tests, not as a substitute for grounding.

The stack should want semantic structure because it can make some work cheaper to inspect, not because it makes meaning magically settled. A good semantic representation reduces the distance between sources, claims, plans, proofs, and explanations. A bad one hides the same uncertainty behind a cleaner shape.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Opaque token prediction does not by itself expose concept hierarchy, source grounding, or interpretable reasoning paths. Yet hand-built ontologies and semantic graphs do not automatically solve grounding either. They can make a system look explainable while encoding the same uncertainties in a different form.

Verification Bandwidth adds a second pressure: a representation must be adequate for the checks it is asked to support. A short semantic token may help routing or retrieval, but it can also lose the constraints needed for verification. Cognitive Compilation adds the operational view: semantic atoms and IR are useful only when typed, validated, and repairable.

This is why hierarchy is not enough. A parent-child relation can support navigation while still failing grounding. A graph traversal can explain a route while still omitting a decisive source constraint. A semantic token can be compact while still too lossy for adjudication. The architecture must test the represented use, not admire the representation.

## Core Claim

[semantic-representation-and-tree-structured-models.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Tree-structured semantic representations should be treated as optional representation substrates that must prove grounding, utility, and interoperability.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The source notes support discussion of semantic graphs, path-derived tokens, graph updates, provenance, traversal explanations, verification limits, and typed semantic IR. They do not support claims that TreeLLM achieves model-size reduction, reasoning gains, or lossless semantic adequacy here.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now carries passage-reviewed mappings for all seven assigned sources. The mappings support optional semantic and cyclic representation substrates with provenance, grounding requirements, typed IR, verification limits, traceability, baseline discipline, and negative-control discipline. They do not support proven TreeLLM model-size reduction, reasoning gain, lossless semantic adequacy, cyclic-model advantage, quality improvement, speed improvement, or context-length improvement in this repository.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `treellm` | Traversable semantic graphs, path-derived fixed-size semantic tokens, residual attributes, graph updates, shared semantic graph usage, analogy/interpolation/counterfactual operations, explanation traces, and editable external knowledge. | No local TreeLLM implementation, measured compression ratio, benchmarked reasoning gain, verified token format, or final-architecture claim exists here. |
| `spinoza` | Proof/citation/procedure-carrying claim graphs, belief revision, contradiction detection, defeaters, downgrade behavior, protected axioms, and verifier-scope limits. | Does not solve open-domain natural-language-to-logic translation or prove semantic graph adequacy here. |
| `verification_bandwidth` | Semantic units as verification objects, verification workspace limits, constraint checks, pairwise grinding, summary loss, and contradiction-rate pressure. | No grounding, context-adequacy, or contradiction-reduction benchmark has been run. |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Typed semantic IR, source plans, semantic atoms, inputs/outputs/constraints/dependencies, compiler passes, validation requirements, localized repair, and target lowering. | No working cognitive compiler, trace suite, or empirical ablation exists in this repository. |
| `cgs` | Compact semantic substrates with compression, generation, residuals, verification, governance, hidden complexity, and permitted use visible. | Does not prove fixed semantic tokens are adequate, grounded, or utility-preserving for any task. |
| `circle_ai_architectures` | Source-note-only optional representation-substrate discipline for phase, recurrence, rotation, sparse cyclic mixing, circular memory, harmonic transforms, or geometry-aware structure with baselines and negative controls. | Raw local project text is not copied here; no universal cyclic-model advantage, model-quality result, sidecar test, Lean build, MLX experiment, or benchmark fixture was run. |
| `coilra_multicoil_rope` | Source-note-only adapter-block indices, residues, winding, relative RoPE laws, circulant mixers, parameter accounting, baselines, wrong-period controls, and explicit non-claims. | Raw local project text is not copied here; no quality, speed, memory, training-stability, or context-length improvement has been shown. |

## Mechanism

Semantic representation is compression with meaning in the loop. TreeLLM proposes explicit traversable semantic graphs and path-derived tokens; Spinoza supplies proof/citation/belief-state discipline; Verification Bandwidth warns that compressed semantic units can lose constraints needed for joint checking; Cognitive Compilation supplies typed semantic IR; CGS and the cyclic representation sources keep representation-substrate claims optional until utility is shown.

A semantic node is a scoped representation lease, not a canonical ontology verdict. It can be used for retrieval, planning, compilation, or explanation only under its grounding state, permitted uses, and adequacy limits. If a task needs constraints the node no longer carries, the route should fall back to richer source context or mark a context-adequacy residual.

A Semantic Node Record can be embedded in semantic pages, claim graphs, or compiler IR without pretending the node is final truth. It records provenance, hierarchy, relation references, grounding state, permitted uses, versioning, residual uncertainty, and evaluation references so that semantic structure remains inspectable rather than authoritative by default.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Source or task evidence"] --> B["Semantic node"]
  B --> C["Parent / child / relation refs"]
  B --> D["Provenance refs"]
  B --> E["Tokenization contract"]
  E --> F{"Grounded enough for use?"}
  F -- "yes" --> G["Adequacy check for task"]
  G --> J{"Adequate?"}
  J -- "yes" --> K["VCM page / claim graph / IR atom"]
  J -- "no" --> H
  F -- "no" --> H["Speculative label + residual"]
  K --> I["Versioned hierarchy"]
  H --> I
```

A semantic representation should preserve:

- Concept identity and label.
- Provenance and permitted-use constraints.
- Parent, child, and relation references.
- Tokenization or traversal contract.
- Grounding state and residual uncertainty.
- Version and supersession links.
- Evaluation references for utility and fidelity.

This lets the stack use tree or graph structure for retrieval, analogy, explanation, compilation, and verification while keeping hierarchy changes auditable.

The three gates are grounding, adequacy, and interoperability. Grounding asks what source or artifact supports the node. Adequacy asks whether the node preserves enough constraints for the current task. Interoperability asks whether the node can be consumed by VCM, Spinoza, planning, compilation, or evidence layers without hidden translation loss. A semantic representation should not be promoted because one gate passed while the others remain untested.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Semantic Node Record.

Minimum fields:

- `node_id`
- `concept_label`
- `provenance_refs`
- `parent_refs`
- `child_refs`
- `relation_refs`
- `tokenization_contract`
- `grounding_state`
- `version`
- `supersedes`
- `residual_uncertainty`
- `permitted_uses`
- `evaluation_refs`

VCM stores semantic pages. Spinoza consumes semantic claims only with evidence and downgrade paths. Cognitive Compilation lowers goals into semantic atoms. Compression layers measure whether semantic tokens preserve enough information for target tasks. Routing treats a representation substrate as optional until tests justify using it.

That interface makes semantic representation a shared boundary rather than a private model feature. If a node is used by a planner, the planner should know its permitted uses and residual uncertainty. If it is used by a proof-carrying claim layer, the layer should see its provenance and downgrade path. If it is used by compression or retrieval, the route should expose which constraints were preserved and which were dropped.

## Invariants

- Semantic nodes are grounded or labeled speculative.
- Hierarchy changes are versioned.
- Representation claims require tests.
- A fixed-size semantic token is not automatically adequate for all tasks.
- Graph updates preserve prior references or record supersession.

The strongest invariant is provenance: a grounded node must say what grounds it. If no provenance exists, the node can still be useful as a hypothesis or planning construct, but it must not masquerade as verified knowledge.

## Failure modes

- Hand-built ontology brittleness.
- False explainability.
- Hierarchy drift.
- Canonical graph capture, where the graph becomes an unreviewed authority source.
- Token compression that drops constraints needed for verification.

Brittleness should trigger residuals and versioning. False explainability should downgrade claims. Hierarchy drift should preserve supersession links. Canonical graph capture should route through governance and audit. Verification loss should be recorded as a context-adequacy failure.

The most subtle failure is authority migration. A semantic graph may begin as a helpful index, then slowly become the thing later agents cite instead of sources, artifacts, or tests. The stack should counter that drift by requiring grounded nodes to keep provenance links active and by treating ungrounded or stale nodes as hypotheses, not as claims.

## Minimal implementation

The smallest useful implementation is a semantic node schema and fixture. The current fixture records provenance, hierarchy, relation references, tokenization contract, grounding state, versioning, supersession, residual uncertainty, permitted uses, and evaluation references.

Passing the fixture does not prove a semantic graph works. It gives the book a stable public contract for later grounding and utility tests.

The next useful implementation step is a small grounded domain where source passages, artifact nodes, semantic nodes, and claim records can be compared. A future test should deliberately update the hierarchy, remove or supersede a source, and check whether downstream uses notice the change. Until then, the schema and Lean predicates show record discipline only, not representation adequacy.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Semantic node record fixture validation | Validate that a semantic node records provenance, hierarchy, relations, tokenization contract, grounding state, versioning, residual uncertainty, permitted uses, and evaluation references. | implemented; passing via `python3 scripts/validate_protocol_examples.py` |
| Grounding fidelity test | Check that grounded nodes have provenance and that task-relevant constraints are preserved. | planned; not run |
| Hierarchy revision test | Check that hierarchy updates preserve prior references or record supersession. | planned; not run |
| Representation utility benchmark | Compare semantic-node representations against neural-only or plain-text baselines on a bounded task. | planned; not run |

The implemented row validates fixture/schema consistency only. The remaining rows require a grounded domain, update operations, baselines, and task metrics; they are not reported results. When a semantic-representation test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, source domain, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:representation.semantic_tree.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.SemanticRepresentation` | A semantic node marked grounded has at least one provenance link. | implemented |
| `lean:representation.semantic_tree.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.SemanticRepresentation` | A hierarchy update preserves prior node references or records supersession. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over semantic-node and hierarchy-update fields. They prove only the record-level gates: a node marked grounded has a positive provenance-link count, and an applied hierarchy update either preserves prior references or records supersession. They do not prove that a graph is unbiased, useful, complete, or benchmark-superior to neural-only representations.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | context_verification_theory | Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `cgs` | Compact Generative Systems | compression_representation | Smallest adequate structure that can generate/govern target without hiding residual complexity. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `circle_ai_architectures` | Circle AI Architectures | cyclic_ai_architecture | Disciplined Circle AI thesis: use phase, recurrence, rotation, sparse cyclic mixing, circular memory, harmonic transforms, or geometry-aware structure only where the structure is real and baselines support it. | source note available |
| `coilra_multicoil_rope` | CoilRA and MultiCoil RoPE | cyclic_mixers_position_encoding | Adapter-block, residue/winding, block-cyclic, multicoil, relative RoPE, circulant convolution, cyclic mixer, and parameter-accounting substrate with explicit non-claims. | source note available |

The crosswalk prevents semantic representation from becoming a free-floating ontology claim. `treellm` supplies the graph/token proposal, `verification_bandwidth` explains why compressed semantic units must expose lost constraints, `cognitive_compilation` supplies typed IR pressure, `spinoza` supplies proof-carrying claim discipline, and the compact/cyclic sources mark optional representation substrates. The chapter remains at `argument` until grounding and utility checks exist.

## Summary

Semantic structure is useful when it makes concepts, paths, provenance, versions, and uncertainty easier to inspect. It is harmful when it becomes an ontology-shaped authority claim without grounding or tests.

Tree-structured models therefore enter the ASI Stack as optional representation substrates: useful for routing, context, compilation, and explanation only where grounding, utility, and interoperability are demonstrated. The next chapter asks how much any of these representation choices should cost, and when the stack must spend more to verify rather than less to answer.

That cost question is not secondary. A representation that saves tokens but increases verification burden, human repair, or downstream failure is not cheaper for the governed system.

The durable principle is that meaning has to stay attached to evidence. Semantic structure can help the stack move faster, but only when each node carries the limits of its own authority.
